data.title: SCAP: Seeking To Reducing Small-Cap Volatility While Generating Higher Income
data.description:
Plenty has been said about the flaws of passive small-cap ETFs that track the Russell 2000. One of the biggest issues is the index’s sheer breadth and relatively loose inclusion criteria, which allow hundreds of “zombie” companies (businesses with little to no profitability) to remain in the mix.

That in turn contributes to another common knock against the index: volatility. Because of their size and weaker fundamentals, many Russell 2000 constituents can swing wildly, making the index much more volatile than large-cap benchmarks like the S&P 500. For some investors, that’s reason enough to avoid small caps altogether.

We believe the InfraCap Small Cap Income ETF (NYSEARCA:SCAP) offers a more thoughtful alternative. By combining actively managed, value-conscious stock selection with the ability to tactically allocate to preferred shares and write options, SCAP is designed to potentially dampen volatility while boosting income.

SCAP: Seeking Lower Volatility

SCAP doesn’t attempt to replicate the full investable universe of U.S. small caps. Instead, the strategy narrows the field by focusing on three attributes that historically correlate with stronger fundamentals and lower volatility.

First is profitability. Some small-cap stocks generate little to no earnings, which adds to their volatility and lowers the reliability of future returns. SCAP, in contrast, targets companies with positive earnings and durable margins. These are businesses with demonstrated financial discipline and a clearer path to compounding returns over time.

Second is the presence of a dividend. Dividend payers are relatively rare in the small-cap space, so identifying them often reveals businesses with healthier cash flows, more mature operations, and shareholder-friendly capital allocation. It also adds an income component to the portfolio that can help cushion volatility.

Finally, SCAP emphasizes valuation. Target holdings must trade at reasonable multiples of both growth and earnings. That avoids speculative small caps with sky-high price-to-sales ratios or structurally unprofitable business models that rely on capital markets to survive.

Taken together, this approach filters out the weakest links and concentrates the portfolio in higher-quality small caps: businesses that are generating cash, returning capital, and not simply surviving on promises.

Over the period shown in the chart below, that quality tilt has translated into consistently lower volatility for SCAP compared to ETFs tracking the Russell 2000 index, even during dislocations like April/May 2025's tariff-induced selloff.

SCAP: Seeking Higher Yield

The Russell 2000 Index isn’t known for its income potential. Many of the small-cap stocks it includes are still in the early stages of development, meaning they often lack the free cash flow or stability required to support a regular dividend. As a result, ETFs tracking this benchmark may only offer a yield of around 1%, give or take.

In contrast, SCAP pays a 6.17% 30-day SEC yield as of 6/30/25. Part of that is due to its dividend-focused screening process, which favors profitable small caps that already return capital to shareholders. But SCAP also structurally enhances its income profile through two additional sources.

The first is options writing. Even though the portfolio itself is actively managed and differs meaningfully from the Russell 2000, SCAP opportunistically writes index options on the Russell 2000 or ETFs to capture volatility premiums. This allows the fund to tap into the higher implied volatility of the benchmark without owning many of the lower-quality names responsible for it.

The second is modest leverage. SCAP can deploy between 10% and 30% notional leverage to amplify exposure to dividend-paying holdings and increase capital allocated to its covered call strategy. This boosts distributable income while maintaining exposure to higher-quality businesses.

While yield isn’t the whole story, SCAP’s advantage isn’t limited to income alone. As shown in the chart below, it also led the Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) on a total return basis over the past year, with distributions reinvested, as of 8/8/25.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. data.title: Bonds Rebound (For Now) As Jobs Outlook Worsens
data.description:
Bonds were all anyone in financial circles could talk about as the post-Labor Day market cranked into gear. The US 30-year Treasury bond was nudging five percent, while UK gilt yields soared past levels last seen in the late 1990s. Stagflation-induced bond weakness was topic number one as the customary Brady Bunch-like panels of talking heads gabbed on CNBC and Bloomberg News. What was the Fed going to do – even if it were to manage to remain independent – when its two tasks of maintaining stable prices and promoting maximum employment were in direct conflict with each other?

Labor Pains

With yesterday’s jobs report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, we probably have a solid answer to that last question, at least for the near term. The August employment numbers validated the sharp downward trend seen in the July report – yes, the one that came out just one month ago with numbers grim enough to put the agency’s head in the crosshairs of a displeased White House (sadly for the administration’s spinmeisters, there is no BLS head available for them to fire this month). In fact, the previous report looks even worse now, with a net negative revision to the June and July numbers. For the month of August, nonfarm payrolls grew by just 22,000 (versus economists’ forecasts of 77,000) and the unemployment rate crept up to 4.3 percent, its highest level since 2021.

The weak jobs report increases the likelihood of a Fed funds rate cut when the Federal Open Market Committee meets the week after next. Market observers see a 0.25 percent rate cut as a near-certainty, barring an unexpectedly red hot inflation print when the Consumer Price Index report comes out next Thursday. And that, in turn, has taken the edge off the bond market mini-freak out earlier this week. Yields have come down across the full spectrum of maturities, with the 2-year Treasury yield now below 3.5 percent, the 10-year at 4.08 percent and the 30-year long bond dropping down below 4.8 percent after that dalliance with five percent earlier in the week. Stock markets are getting an injection of rate cut-driven animal spirits as well, with the S&P 500 up half a percent just after yesterday’s market open

Yes, But Stagflation

The short-term palliative of rate cuts may not last for long. We won’t have a full inflation picture until next week’s CPI and PPI reports come out, but last Friday’s Personal Consumption Expenditures report (the inflation number most closely watched by the Fed) showed core PCE ticking up to 2.9 percent, still far away from the Fed’s two percent target rate. As consumer-facing companies wrap up their second quarter earnings reports, more and more of them are affirming that the days of finagling supply chains and inventory levels are coming to an end. Consumers will be seeing more of the higher costs brought about by tariffs, and may finally understand that tariffs are, in effect, a sales tax.

So despite the late-week reprieve in yields, we will not be surprised to see the 30-year bond head back up towards five percent and perhaps wind up on the other side of that round number threshold. Of still greater concern is that this trend is happening at the same time that the US dollar is weakening against other currencies. One of the most dependable trends in financial markets over the years has been the positive correlation between the dollar and the long bond yield. The April 2 tariff announcement broke that trend, and it hasn’t recovered since.

Normally, rising yields on what is supposed to be the world’s safest asset makes that asset more attractive and thus boosts the value of the dollar as investors pile in. A weak dollar, on the other hand, suggests that the brand value of that “world’s safest asset” nomenclature is not what it used to be, and the potential for stagflation threatens to push yields even higher. A cut to the Fed funds rate – or even successive cuts as the more dovish FOMC members want to see – is not by itself going to offset the threats to long-term yields brought into the spotlight earlier this week. Caution remains the watchword when it comes to long duration exposures.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Upstart May Be A Future Victim Of AI - Downgrade To Sell
data.description:
Upstart (UPST) looks like a potential winner from a shift in interest rate policy, as its loan business might see some tailwinds to demand. The company has finally returned to GAAP profitability as well as the favored “beat and raise” cadence. At the same time, however, I am growing concerned about potential volatility from tariffs and a weakening economy and how that is not reflected in the stock’s valuation. I am downgrading the stock to a sell rating.

UPST Stock Price

I last covered UPST in June, where I downgraded the stock on account of valuation. The stock has fallen around 9% since then.

The company’s results were very strong, but that, ironically, might be the problem.

UPST Stock Key Metrics

UPST is a financial technology company that uses artificial intelligence to originate loans. The company positions itself as an alternative to traditional credit scoring models like FICO and potentially being able to allow banks to make loan investments to borrowers that they otherwise would have turned away. The company is back on track with its projected return profile at a comfortable spread to the US Treasury yields.

In the most recent quarter, UPST “delivered the goods.” The company generated 102% YoY revenue growth to $257 million, crushing guidance of $225 million.

The company posted a 58% contribution margin, representing 300 bps of sequential expansion and beating guidance of 55%. I had previously noted potential risks from declining contribution margins.

On the profitability front, the company delivered $53 million of adjusted EBITDA, crushing guidance of $37 million.

The company even delivered positive GAAP net income, one quarter earlier than expected.

The company has seen its committed capital partnerships continue to grow. The lack of funding was one important negative catalyst when the stock crashed in 2021 through 2022.

The company however did not see its loans held on its balance sheet decline from the first quarter - they even increased to $1.02 billion (albeit with the majority still being for “R&D” purposes).

Looking ahead, management has guided for the company to see $280 million of revenue in the third quarter, representing 73% YoY growth. Management has guided for contribution margins of 58%, perhaps the contraction seen earlier in the year is reversing. I note that the company will begin lapping tough comparables in the fourth quarter.

On the conference call, management noted that a decline in interest rates would be a significant catalyst due to improving conversion rates. Management noted that their strategy in their home and auto lending products isn’t necessarily about price but is instead about providing “a differentiated experience.” Management noted that they plan to transition funding for these two products to third-party capital by “the end of the year.” I wonder if this took some investors by surprise, in a negative way, who may have been hoping for faster progress on moving to external funding (for example, for the core personal lending business).

Is UPST Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, UPST traded hands at around 6x sales. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain rapid growth even beyond this year.

Based on long term net margins of around 20% to 35%, that places the valuation anywhere between 17x and 30x earnings, or 11x to 20x 2027e earnings. Investors might be excited that management’s notes regarding a potential acceleration in the business might come to fruition on future interest rate cuts, but I have a more sobering outlook. The rise of generative AI appears to have spurred investor interest in UPST, given the company’s use of AI for its algorithms. The bullish thesis centers around UPST being some sort of loan origination operating system for banks around the world. But the problem is that as generative AI becomes more and more easily accessible, I expect banks to improve their own loan origination capabilities through AI themselves.

Those banks would have a cost advantage due to low-cost deposits, something that UPST does not have (and I remind readers that UPST is still a ways away from being able to fund its personal loans using outside capital). Whereas UPST is trading at a big premium to typical financial stocks, it is not clear at all whether that premium is deserved, as the main bullish thesis appears to have broken down. The company is seeing strong results, but arguably deserves to trade at the typical cyclical multiples carried at banks and other lending providers.

Maybe not quite so low, given the company does not have deposit-fleeing risk (courtesy of having no deposits at all), but the company is still not yet at an asset-light business model. I can see the stock re-rating downwards to around 3x sales, implying a valuation of around 8.5x to 15x earnings. That presents some downside over the coming years, even after accounting for growth.

UPST Stock Conclusion

The fact that UPST stock had fallen immediately following the earnings report, in spite of beating and raising guidance by a huge margin, is, in my view, evidence of shifting sentiment. It is always difficult to judge when re-ratings might happen, and this explains why I have previously expressed my bearishness more in terms of neutrality. But with this potential shift in sentiment coinciding with potential volatility from tariffs, I am now of the view that it is time to take a more firm stance. I am downgrading the stock to a sell rating.

data.title: JSI: Actively Managed Securitized Investment ETF, Above-Average Yield, Strong Performance Track Record
data.description:
The Janus Henderson Securitized Income ETF (JSI) is an actively managed ETF focusing on high-quality, short-term securitized assets, including MBS, ABS, and CLOs. JSI's above-average 6.0% dividend yield, strong performance track record, and below-average volatility, make the fund a buy. JSI is similar to several other ETFs I've recently covered, including the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE) and the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY). There are few significant differences between these funds, with all being strong investment opportunities, and buys.

JPIE - Overview and Investment Thesis

Portfolio

JSI is an actively-managed ETF investing in securitized assets, mostly mortgage-backed securities, or MBS, but with significant investments in other types of assets. Allocations are as follows:

JSI's investments are securitized products, a specific type of investment product with specific characteristics. By investing in securitized products, the fund has indirect exposure/investment in other assets. As an example, by investing in mortgage-backed securities, MBS, the fund is indirectly investing in thousands of mortgages. By investing in CLOs, the fund has indirect exposure to senior loans. Securitized products behave a bit differently from their underlying investments, generally only a little bit, sometimes significantly (although I don't think there are many examples of this in JSI's holdings).

The extra layer here adds a bit of complexity to these assets, but the securitization process is meant to improve these products, and I do believe that it generally does. Not always, with the past financial crisis being a gigantic exception, but generally.

JSI is a reasonably well-diversified fund, with investments in a couple of different bond sub-asset classes, direct investments in over 400 securities, indirect investments in thousands. It is definitely less diversified than some of its peers, including the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC), which invests in a broader assortment of fixed-income securities. Some might prefer BINC's more diversified holdings, but JSI's portfolio is diversified enough, in my opinion at least.

Risk and Volatility

JSI focuses on investment-grade securities, with these accounting for more than 3/4 of its portfolio. Non-investment grade securities, and those without ratings, account for the remainder.

JSI further focuses on short-term securities, with an average duration of 2.7 years. Duration is slightly higher than most short-term fixed-income indexes, quite a bit lower than broader indexes.

The combination of low credit and rate risk should ensure a reasonably stable share price regardless of macro conditions. Expect some losses during downturns and recessions, some losses when rates rise too, but not significant ones. JSI's significant MBS could plausibly lead to gains during recessions, as rates tend to plummet during these, but I doubt it. Duration is too low, and treasuries are the asset to hold during a recession (most ones, at least).

JSI's price is indeed more stable than that of broader bond indexes, including both investment and non-investment grade. Volatility seems broadly comparable to indexes tracking short-term bonds, as expected.

JSI should experience comparatively lower drawdowns too. As the fund was created in late 2023, it has yet to experience any sort of adverse market event, so we can't really gauge its performance during one. Drawdowns have been quite low since inception, although this does not tell us much.

JSI invests quite heavily in TBA MBS, basically MBS futures. Futures are generally leveraged, with JSI itself using leverage for around 26% of its net assets. See the negative cash position at the bottom.

As the accounting on derivatives and futures is somewhat messy, definitely complicated, said leverage does not necessarily result in increased risk or volatility for a fund. JSI definitely does not have an above-average level of volatility for a bond ETF, as previously mentioned. Volatility is generally between that of short-term investment-grade/non-investment-grade bonds, in line with an unleveraged fund with JSI's characteristics. Volatility increased a couple of weeks ago though, and remains a bit higher than expected right now.

In any case, JSI focuses on comparatively low-risk, low-volatility securities relative to peers, and its performance is consistent with that. This is a significant, straightforward benefit for the fund and its shareholders, and core part of its investment thesis. The leverage does cut against this, but overall volatility remains low.

Dividend Yield and Performance Analysis

JSI yields 6.0%, quite a bit more than most bonds, slightly lower than high-yield bonds. It is a reasonably good yield on an absolute basis, even better accounting for the fund's below-average level of risk and volatility. Investors get a yield close to high-yield bonds, but with credit quality closer to investment-grade.

JSI's performance track-record is reasonably good, with the fund outperforming most bonds and treasuries since inception, matching the performance of investment-grade corporate bonds, underperforming riskier high-yield bonds. Interest rates have slightly declined since inception, an unfavorable development for JSI versus its peers. Credit spreads have declined too, so the higher underperformance to high-yield bonds is to be expected. Results here imply long-term underperformance, unless rates continuously trend downwards from here on out.

Overall, JSI has slightly outperformed fixed-income markets since inception with below-average risk, and during slightly unfavorable economic conditions. Seems good to me.

Peer Comparison and Other Thoughts

As mentioned previously, JSI is quite similar to several other ETFs I've recently covered. Portfolios and overall characteristics are quite similar, with these ETFs all focusing on short-term, investment-grade securities, with above-average dividends and returns, below-average risk and volatility.

JSI focuses much more strongly on securitized assets than most of these, which tend to have somewhat more diversified portfolios. BINC is probably the most diversified of the bunch, CARY the closest to JSI. JSI has the most leverage, with most of these ETFs having either zero or small single-digit amounts. Said leverage has had negligible impact on the fund's performance compared to these ETFs, so far:

As mentioned previously, sometimes derivative leverage is simply an accounting issue, which seems to be the case for JSI. Credit quality and rate risk for these ETFs are quite similar, so leverage should lead to higher volatility for JSI, but that does not seem to be the case.

More broadly, I'm actually surprised at how close their returns have been. CARY is quite similar, so returns here are to be expected, but BINC's portfolio is definitely a bit different, and that seems to have had a negligible effect. Asset allocations:

As can be seen above, BINC has much higher investments in assorted corporate bonds, much lower leverage, but effectively identical returns. I'm not entirely sure why this is the case, perhaps a combination of efficient markets and the fact that JSI is a young ETF (returns were not always so similar).

In any case, the above makes me slightly more bullish on BINC over the alternatives. BINC is the most diversified, so safest in a way, cheaper than most too, with a 0.40% expense ratio compared to 0.49% for JSI. Of course these advantages have not led to outperformance in the past, and might not do so in the future.

As an aside, one of the reasons these ETFs all get buy ratings from me is that they are quite similar, and it isn't trivially easy to pick the 'best' one.

Conclusion

JSI's above-average 6.0% dividend yield, strong performance track record, and below-average volatility, make the fund a buy.

data.title: QinetiQ: After A 25% Rally, What's Next For Defense Investors?
data.description:
QinetiQ (QNTQY) (QNQTF), a defense technology provider, saw its share prices surge by almost 25%, outperforming the S&P 500’s 9.2% gain and perfectly aligned with my buy rating. The stock price benefits from positive momentum for European defense stocks, but at the same time we note that the company ran into issues in FY25 and sees little growth in sales for FY26. In this report, I review the company’s buy rating and update my price target.

What Does QinetiQ Group Do?

QinetiQ Group, headquartered in the UK, was established in 2001 after the privatization of the Defense Evaluation and Research Agency. The company operates through two segments:

  • EMEA Services covering sales in Europe, the Middle East, and Australasia
  • Global Solutions, which provides products and services to customers in the US, UK, and Australasia.

The company is not a pure-play aerospace and defense company serving a wide range of industries and markets:

  • Aerospace and defence
  • Marine
  • Government and Law Enforcement
  • Energy
  • Telecommunications
  • Financial Services

The company excels in test and evaluation for air, land, sea, and target systems. The company is also positioned well for parts of the defense spectrum that are becoming increasingly more important, such as cybersecurity, training, mission data and innovation, robotics, and autonomous systems.

QinetiQ is not a company that builds the next fighter jet or missile defense systems, instead it is focused more on providing defense services, testing, and technology solutions. It is positioned as a specialized defense technology and services contractor.

QinetiQ Had A Rough FY25 Despite Major Defense Growth Cycle

Many aerospace and defense companies are seeing good growth in their financials. QinetiQ, however, was not one of those companies. In FY25, the company was plagued by sales slipping due to administration changes in the US and the UK. Furthermore, the company’s integration of Avantus has been more challenging than anticipated. QinetiQ is a rather small defense player in the US, so they lack the scale to compete. As part of a restructuring, the company has reached an agreement with V2X (VVX) to sell a non-core business for $31 million.

This should provide the company better opportunities to benefit from several global tailwinds:

  • A $1 trillion defense and security budget in the US.
  • A target set by the UK to reach a defense budget of 2.5% GDP by 2030.
  • Defense spending of 2.4% of GDP in Australia.

We keep in mind that it has three functional currencies (GBP, USD, AUD), and that could provide some currency risk.

QinetiQ Guidance For FY26 Is Not Impressive

The guidance that QinetiQ issued for FY26 is not one that would give the impression that the company is benefiting from major growth in the defense market. Revenues are expected to grow just 3%, and profit margins will be around 11%. Essentially, the margin that the company is targeting is simply regrowing the cost efficiency to prior levels before the business in the US became a drag. The company also noted that the short-cycle businesses, which are contracts with a shorter duration and faster revenue recognition, remain challenging.

QinetiQ does not provide detailed quarterly reports, instead it provides trading updates with a more detailed report every six months. In the most recent quarterly update, the company confirmed its full-year targets with 46 to 48 percent of revenue generation in the first half of the year at 10% margins and acceleration of sales and margins in the second half of the year. This is consistent with the backloaded profile we see for many companies in aerospace and defense.

Buy Opportunity Remains Despite Low Growth For QinetiQ Group Stock

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

As I previously noted, QinetiQ has two ADRS:

  • QNTQY representing four ordinary shares.
  • QNTQF representing one ordinary share.

Both of these tickers lack significant trading volumes, which may provide challenges to buying and selling at desired prices and volumes. Therefore, if you are interested in investing in QinetiQ, I recommend checking out the listing at the London Stock Exchange, where the company trades under the ticker QQ.

While it is not the case that the problems in the US legacy business occurred overnight, keeping an eye on the overall trajectory of the company is more important than ever to assess upside. Previously, the company had major upside simply because European defense companies were trading at a steep discount. While many companies continue to trade at a discount compared to global aerospace and defense peers, the discount against the median multiples for the companies has significantly tightened. So, many companies either rely on earnings growth or multiple expansion or a combination thereof.

For QinetiQ, we note the low growth of sales while the share price has increased substantially. This necessitates a careful modelling of the expected results to assess the existence of any upside.

My prior target for QinetiQ Group stock was $24.95. I am updating that target to $29.43, representing 14% upside from current levels.

The following items drove the increase in the price target:

  • An increase in the median EV/EBITDA for QinetiQ Group.
  • FY25 EBITDA being 9.7% higher than previously anticipated, with around 3.5%-4% upward revision for FY26 and FY27.
  • A higher free cash flow generation in FY25, partially offset by lower free cash flow generation in FY26 and FY27.
  • The sale of a non-core business driving proceeds of $31 million.

So, we see that EBITDA estimates improved, free cash flow estimates declined somewhat but were substantially stronger than anticipated in the realized results. The fact remains that QinetiQ Group is not a high-growth company, but even with that in mind, we note that the company has 14% upside against an EV/EBITDA multiple that is substantially below that of its peers.

Conclusion: QinetiQ Stock Remains Attractive Despite Low Growth

When I saw the stock price increase since my last report and the low growth expected this year, my concern was that a lot of growth had already been factored into the stock price. That, however, is not the case. FY26 earnings are not yet reflected in the stock price, and valuing the stock one year ahead of earnings (meaning pulling FY27 into the target), we see appealing upside for a company that may also benefit from further multiple expansion if it grows its margins and business further.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Winning With CTSIX: 3 Potentially Powerful Drivers
data.description:
By Brandon Nelson, CFA

We asked Senior Portfolio Manager Brandon Nelson to share what’s driven Calamos Timpani Small Cap Growth Fund’s (CTSIX) performance and his thoughts about the fund’s prospects. Key points include:

  • CTSIX is up 15.32% year to date through August 31, 2025, more than 800 basis points ahead of the Russell 2000 Growth Index and more than 1000 basis points ahead of the Morningstar US Small Cap Growth Fund peer group average.
  • Individual security selection has driven returns year to date1—what Senior Portfolio Manager Brandon Nelson calls “winning for the right reasons.”
  • Nelson sees three drivers for investors to potentially win with CTSIX in the future—the fund’s fundamental momentum investment process, its security selection, and the prospect for a rebound in the small-cap asset class.

Figure 1. CTSIX Has Delivered Outperformance Over the Short and Long Term

Q. Why do you believe investors should consider CTSIX?

A. CTSIX has historically provided strong performance through many periods, which I hope gives confidence that our process is repeatable. I believe there are three ways investors can potentially win with CTSIX:

  1. The fund utilizes an investment process—fundamental momentum—that has tended to be well-received by investors.
  2. We’re focused on finding favorable and unique company-specific situations within that fundamental momentum framework.
  3. We believe there’s a rebound on the horizon for an asset class that is temporarily down and out.

Q. Tell us more about how you select stocks based on fundamental momentum.

A. For us, fundamental momentum means two things: sustainably fast growth and underestimated growth. We want companies that have an open-ended growth opportunity in front of them—typically a situation where they have low but quickly rising market share of a large market.

The underestimated growth portion of fundamental momentum tends to relate more to management’s ability to successfully manage expectations for investors. We seek companies beating expectations quarter after quarter—whether that’s three quarters, three years, or more. The market tends to reward stocks beating expectations with higher and higher valuations.

I would rather buy a stock where the sustained growth is expected to be 15% and they consistently report 23% than buying a stock where the sustained growth is 35% but they consistently report 30% growth. A company has to have both sustainably fast growth and upside to growth.

Q. Let’s turn to your second point—finding favorable and unique situations.

A. This is the idiosyncratic layer where we spend all of our time—digging deep with company management teams and our network of contacts. The small-cap market is inefficient, sometimes creating misaligned stock prices. There aren’t as many eyeballs looking at these stocks. But if you get the right small caps—that can be really exciting. We believe CTSIX’s results show that we’ve done a good job of that over the life of the fund.

Idiosyncratic security selection has been the main driver of outperformance this year, and it’s not just one or two names driving performance. We have a nice variety of names putting points on board—in health care, consumer discretionary, financials, technology, and industrials. To me, this means we’ve won for the right reasons.

We’re investing with conviction but also staying diversified, which has historically been a winning formula for us. Most of our investments are one-off situations, but we’ve also found some compelling themes in AI infrastructure, online gambling, and corporate reshoring in response to tariffs and recent legislation.

Q. What do you say to the investor who says, “small caps have underperformed for years, why bother?”

A. I get this question a lot. I think a lot of investors who aren’t in small caps today will look back and see this as a missed opportunity. Consider:

  • Small caps have generally won. It may not have been the case over the past 10 years or so, but if we look back over much longer time periods, small and large caps alternate, and small caps have led large caps overall.

Figure 2. Small has often outperformed big: Big versus small since 1928

  • We believe rotation is overdue. While the past doesn’t predict the future, history can provide useful context. According to research from Jefferies published this spring, small caps have been on their longest losing streak ever, at more than 8.4 years, considerably longer than the average down period of 4.4 years.2
  • When the baton pass has happened, small caps have come out of downturns with jaw-dropping strength—on average, outperforming large caps over the subsequent one-year period by more than 27%, according to the same Jefferies study. I believe that’s a reason to be in small caps ahead of the turn.
  • Valuations for small caps are very attractive, in my view, on both an absolute basis and relative to large caps. Most stocks these days are being valued on 2026 earnings, and it’s fascinating how linear this relationship is. The largest 300 stocks are trading at about 20 times estimated earnings, and the micro caps are at only 12 times.

Figure 3. Smaller stocks, better-priced growth prospects

  • Mathematically, research shows that big winners can disproportionately come from the small and micro buckets, even when small caps aren’t leading in the market. From 2013 to 2023, 77% of huge winners (1000+%) still came from the small and micro cap space.3

Q. Any closing thoughts for investors?

A. I hope that CTSIX’s results and our approach make it easy for investors to build strategic allocations to small caps. Our fundamental investment process has served us well historically, and it’s encouraging to see the market is paying more attention to company-specific growth drivers. From a bottom-up standpoint, I’m really excited about CTSIX’s portfolio—it’s stacked with small and micro caps that check all the boxes for our team. While we’ve demonstrated we don’t need the small cap asset class to win, there are many reasons to believe that a baton pass from large to small is coming into focus, which could give our holdings an additional boost.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

1Source: Calamos Investments as of August 31, 2025. Active Return: Difference between the benchmark and the portfolio return. Idiosyncratic: The portion of active returns attributable to sources not explained by common factors, often referred to as “stock-specific.”

2Source: Steven DeSanctis, Jefferies. The performance is calculated (or derived) based on data from the CRSP cap-weighted database. Source: Jefferies using Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP©), The University of Chicago Booth School of Business, Jefferies. Data from January 1926 through May 31, 2025.

3Source: FactSet, Raymond James Research. Period from 2013-2023, 10-Year 1000+ returners. Small cap: 52%; micro cap; 25%: mid cap, 17%; large cap, 6%.

Opinions, estimates, forecasts, and statements of financial market trends that are based on current market conditions constitute our judgment and are subject to change without notice. The views and strategies described may not be appropriate for all investors. References to specific securities, asset classes and financial markets are for illustrative purposes only and are not intended to be, and should not be interpreted as, recommendations.

Diversification and asset allocation do not guarantee a profit or protect against a loss. Alternative strategies entail added risks and may not be appropriate for all investors. Indexes are unmanaged, not available for direct investment and do not include fees and expenses.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Veeva Systems: Competition Intensifying, Shares Remain Overvalued
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Andres Veurink as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Veeva Systems Inc. (VEEV) is a cloud-based platform offering several data management and software services to the life science industry, including support for clinical trial documents, quality management systems, and regulatory submissions and reporting. The industry is expected to grow at a steady pace, and VEEV has the opportunity to take more market share.

However, with impressive growth often comes higher valuations. With VEEV, they are exceeding what I am comfortable with. My models show a clear overvaluation even if VEEV, in the event of drastically increasing net margins, can increase in price even more. Shares are not a buy, but holding on to them to “ride the wave” seems like a decent route to go right now. The company's internal figures are all showing improvement, so a sell rating would be too aggressive. So my rating is Hold.

Stock Chart 3-Year Performance

I did a comparison between VEEV and the BVP Nasdaq Emerging Cloud Index (EMCLOUD). EMCLOUD is an index comprised of cloud and software companies. It is not just for US-based companies but has a global reach. VEEV might be in a different niche than other companies, but I still find it highly relevant to get a comparison to the "average," hence the index.

VEEV has outperformed the index, sitting at a 32.48% 3-year return compared to a 21.76% return. What I value here is that VEEV has had lower volatility as well, with a 5-year monthly sitting at 0.93. At times, the index was outperforming VEEV, but since it posted strong earnings after beating estimates and raising full-year guidance, it has regained and has yielded a higher overall return.

Product Analysis

VEEV is a digital service provider focusing on the global life science industry. Its products include cloud-based software aimed at improving efficiency and management for both small-scale and large-scale companies.

Its Veeva Commercial Cloud offers management solutions in several areas like execution, sales, marketing, and medical teams. Its Vault CRM, for example, offers customer profiling services, and Veeva Vault PromoMats focuses on claims management. The Veeva Vault platform supports over 50 applications. Throughout its several offerings, VEEV has implemented AI in the form of highly advanced language models, which help with improving efficiency, managing projects, and decision-making.

Target Audience

The target audience is pharmaceutical companies, biotechnology firms, and medical device manufacturers, including Pfizer Inc. (PFE), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Novartis AG (NVS), and Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK). Recently, VEEV has also entered the food and beverage industry by providing services that check the safety and quality of food items, extending its Vault Quality Suite that serves pharmaceutical clients to the food industry. This opens up a completely new market for VEEV. The need for increased regulatory documentation and compliance for the food industry is increasing, showing a growing need for the services VEEV offers. Food safety testing is likely to compound 7.85% annually to $44 billion by 2032. This is especially exciting since nearly 50% of the industry is in the US, right where VEEV is also located.

According to TowardsHealthCare the global life science industry will be worth $70 billion in 2034, compounding at an annual rate of 12.94%. The life science software industry is expected to reach $27.5 billion in value, according to AppsRunTheWorld, representing a CAGR of 5.9% from 2024. In that report, it's included that VEEV ranks among the top three with an estimated market share of 13%. TTM revenues for VEEV are $2.968 billion, which gives it a 14.4% market share based on the 2024 market value of $20.6 billion. The report's estimates seem to be accurate based on these comparisons.

Scalability

In a sensitivity table we can see the impact of VEEV increasing its market share on revenues based on the TMV (Total Market Value) in 2029, as cited earlier.

The input figure is $27.5 billion, in line with the reporting on 2029 market value. The table includes assumptions of VEEV both losing a small percentage of market share and also gaining some, ranging between 12% and 22%. The net margin assumptions range between 27% and 37%, with the lower amount representing the TTM figure VEEV now has. I use this number because I believe VEEV to be in a scaling phase where net margins will be easily retainable due to higher user growth and consolidating OPEX. Over the past 3 years, VEEV has significantly slowed down its hiring process. At the same time, revenues have increased strongly as subscription revenues are beginning to compound at a faster rate from new customer acquisitions. This speaks to VEEV's scalability.

Referring to the table, it shows a net income range of $861 million to $2.2 billion, with the largest change coming from obtaining a higher market share rather than increasing net margins. From this result, I am going to base future assumptions on valuation far more on customer growth rather than margin growth.

Qualities of Revenues and Growth Sensitivity Analysis

The revenue distribution for VEEV is heavily skewed towards subscription revenues, making up 84.7% of the 2026 guided revenues. This theme has remained throughout the past 5 years. During 2021 the percentage was 80.4%. The increase has meant a large increase in net income due to the lower COGS and OPEX related to subscription revenues in general.

During Q2, VEEV recorded $659 million in subscription revenues and $129 million in “professional service and other.” The cost of revenues was $90.8 million for subscriptions and $86.4 million for professional services and other. This gives each revenue source a cost of goods sold as a percentage of sales of 13.77% and 66.9%. This shows that as subscription revenues grow, it will drastically increase gross margins since the percentage is so much lower.

The sensitivity table is looking at subscription revenues in 2030 with an input value of $3.744 billion. I arrive at this figure by taking the forecasted 2026 subscription revenues and applying a very conservative 7% annual growth rate to it.

The two inputs are then revenue growth and COGS as a percentage of sales. The table shows that higher COGS have a significant impact on gross profits even as revenues increase as a whole. At the right side, percentage difference is 11.1% between the bottom and top. Looking at COGS, I believe, is crucial with a SaaS business like VEEV because maintaining incredibly low percentages here will have a significant impact on the rest of the business. SaaS businesses often trade at a higher P/E due to the lower COGS, which provides a cushion of sorts for the bottom line. They generally also receive a higher valuation because once subscriptions are in, it normally remains quite stable. This is reflected, for instance, in the full-year guidance, where VEEV has a $6 million difference between the high end and low end of revenues.

Furthermore, in the terms and conditions for VEEV, they have a 90-day minimum contract termination. Customers also sign contracts for a 1-year period at a time.

Multiple Valuation

With a sensitivity table utilizing price-to-earnings multiples at different levels compared to net margins at different percentages, we can visualize the share price for VEEV. The input for the table is revenues of $3.14 billion, in line with guidance. Shares outstanding used are 162 million. I’ve color-formatted the table to show green when above 250-300, and red when below 250-200. It clearly shows no green cells, even with very generous inputs like 30x earnings and 37% net margins at the lower right end. This shows me that shares are likely heavily overpriced right now. TTM net margins are also 27%, meaning that even if VEEV achieves an impressive margin expansion, it still won't put it over the line. I make the case that it's a hold still because of the strong internal growth in the company; it would seem wrong to rate it a buy when the margins have actually been improving over time.

Because of what the table shows me and the intense competition right now in the industry, I wouldn’t want to be an investor. The biggest competitors include Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), Waystar Holding Corp. (WAY) and HealthEquity, Inc. (HQY). The second and third are sizable — smaller than VEEV but still posing a threat as their capital allocations reflect aggressive expansion to gain market.

Shares trade at a high premium even for a growth company. Salesforce, for instance, which has been around longer and is more mature, trades at 22x FWD earnings. At that multiple and the highest net margins in the table, it gives me a target price of $157, 58% below current trading levels.

Risks

The biggest competition to VEEV is Salesforce. The company has been investing heavily in building out its own life science cloud platforms, focusing on customer relationship management. In 2025, Salesforce secured a partnership with Takeda, one of the largest pharmaceutical companies in the industry. The partnerships include Takeda using the Salesforce platform for customer management using the Salesforce Life Sciences Cloud product.

Besides this, VEEV is currently undergoing a migration of customers from its CRM operations on the Salesforce platform to its own proprietary platform, Veeva Vault Platform. When questioned on the matter, management explained that so far 20 customers have completed the transition, but there are still 300 more to go. The slow progress seems to be from hesitant customers, which doesn't help assure shareholders that future subscription revenues will be as strong as now.

The Full Picture

The truth is that VEEV has grown at an impressive rate and has secured several industry titans as customers. Margins could continue to tick up more as subscription revenues continue to compound. However, increasing competition in the industry, along with a very steep valuation, makes me have little conviction about the long-term prospects of a position.

data.title: Principal Well-Being Index: Small Businesses Not Yet Increasing Prices
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Percentage of firms that reported increasing prices for their customers

Principal Well-Being Index

The latest Principal Well-Being Index showed that, as tariffs have risen, smaller businesses have been restrained in increasing prices — likely amid a focus on preserving market share.

The Principal Well-Being Index tracks sentiment across small- and medium-sized businesses by asking eight questions about current conditions and the economic outlook. Now in its twelfth year, the latest survey was conducted from July 10 to 28. It gathered responses from 1,000 businesses, comparable in scale to the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index.

Survey results revealed that smaller businesses are more likely to absorb cost increases from higher tariffs than larger businesses. A combination of inventory run-down, elevated profit margins, and a desire to protect market share is adding incentives for small businesses to shoulder higher input prices, at least for now. Trade policy uncertainty has also created decision paralysis, leading to a "wait-and-see" mode among small businesses. Overall, these factors have likely contributed to the weaker tariff pass-through effect observed so far.

However, with higher tariffs only taking effect in August, smaller businesses are still in the early stages of adjustment. As profit cushions erode, the trade-off between preserving market share and protecting margins will sharpen, making tariff pass-through to consumers not just possible but increasingly probable. The coming quarters may reveal whether small businesses can continue to absorb shocks — or whether inflation pressures re-emerge through consumer prices.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The S&P 500, Dow And Nasdaq Since 2000 Highs As Of August 2025
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500, Dow Jones Industrial Average (Dow), and Nasdaq Composite are all stock market indexes used to measure the performance of various aspects of the U.S. stock market. The indexes generally rise and fall together; however, the extent of gains or losses produced by each can differ depending on market conditions and the state of the economy.

The indexes differ in several key ways such as weighting methods, coverage, and criteria for including stocks. The S&P 500 assigns weightings based on market capitalization, includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors, and offers a more comprehensive view of the broad market's performance. The Nasdaq also employs market cap weighting but includes over 3,000 stocks with a heavy focus on the technology sector, making it a popular benchmark for technology and growth companies. In contrast, the Dow is a smaller index consisting of 30 well-established "blue-chip" stocks, with weightings based on stock prices, making it a more conservative and limited representation of the broader market.

In this article, we examine these three indices and how they have changed since their peaks from 2000. We've updated the data through the August 2025 close.

Here are two overlays — one with the nominal price, excluding dividends, and the other with the price adjusted for inflation based on the consumer price index for urban consumers (which is usually just referred to as the CPI). At the end of August, the S&P 500 finished up 1.9%, the Dow 30 finished up 3.2%, and the Nasdaq finished up 1.6% from July.

When adjusted for inflation, the real month-over-month changes for each index become 1.8% for the S&P 500, 3.0% for the Dow 30, and 1.4% for the Nasdaq.

The charts require little explanation. The first 15 years of the 21st century was not kind to equity investors. However, the market has bounced back over the last 10 years with each index growing 124% (S&P 500), 106% (Dow), and 125% (Nasdaq) in real terms.

ETF Performance

Performance of the SPY ETF Since 2000 Peak

The SPY ETF is designed to track the performance of the S&P 500. Let's take a look at the return on $1,000 invested in the SPY ETF at its March 2000 peak.

The total return certainly looks better over 25 years later, but the real (inflation-adjusted) purchasing power of that $1,000 is currently $3,499, a real compounded annual return of 5.05%.

Performance of the DIA ETF Since 2000 Peak

The DIA ETF is designed to track the performance of the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Now, let's take a look at the return on $1,000 invested in the DIA ETF at its January 2000 peak.

Again, the total return looks better over 25 years later, but the real purchasing power of that $1,000 is currently $3,515, a real compounded annual return of 5.03%.

Performance of the QQQ ETF Since 2000 Peak

The QQQ ETF is designed to track the performance of the Nasdaq-100 Index. In our last chart, let's look at the return on $1,000 invested in the QQQ ETF at its March 2000 peak.

The real purchasing power of that $1,000 is currently $3,113, a real compounded annual return of 4.56%.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Covenant Logistics: Record Revenue In A Weak Cycle, But A Stretched Balance Sheet
data.description:
CVLG Against the Trucking Cycle

The trucking sector remains under pressure. The Dow Jones U.S. Trucking Index is down more than 16% over the past year, reflecting weaker volumes and contract rates stuck at cycle lows. While the S&P 500 keeps moving higher, mid-cap carriers like Covenant Logistics (CVLG) have been dragged down by industry weakness. Over the last twelve months, CVLG is off about 9%, better than its sector index but still lagging the broader market.

Freight indicators such as the Cass Freight Index and DAT Freight & Analytics confirm the same picture, a market with limited pricing power and lingering overinvestment in fleets since 2021 that hasn’t fully corrected yet. On top of that, structural costs keep rising. According to the EIA, diesel prices are up 15% year-to-date, while BLS data shows driver wages and insurance expenses at record highs.

My view, after reviewing both the financials and the sector backdrop, is to maintain a neutral stance. Covenant delivered solid Q2 revenues and beat EPS expectations, but the balance sheet remains stretched. The company closed the quarter with just $0.14M in cash and relies heavily on its $110M credit revolver, with $65.5M still available. The $35M in buybacks helped EPS but pushed net debt up to $268.7M, leaving leverage at 2x EBITDA.

At the current share price of $23.88, CVLG trades at 6.4x EV/EBITDA, broadly in line with peers. A more bullish case would require EBITDA moving up toward $150M, which would signal improving operating ratios and stronger rates, a first sign of tailwinds returning to the sector. Until then, the most reasonable stance is Hold, waiting for those catalysts to justify a higher multiple and a breakout from the current trading range of $17–26.

A Diversified Model Still Led by the Founder

In the 1980s, Covenant was founded in Chattanooga as an expedite operator. It is still run by the same founder, David Parker, which explains why the company prioritizes growth in verticals that create long-term value. And also why, in a weak cycle like the current one, the company maintains an active buyback policy.

CVLG’s model is based on defined verticals and, in my view, well diversified to cushion the weakness of general freight and provide more stability.

First is the Expedited vertical, which operates with about 860 tractors in a just-in-time model focused mainly on transporting auto parts. The operating ratio is tight due to the sensitivity of the business, as it depends heavily on the cycle and on the availability of drivers. In Q2 the OR stood at 94%.

The largest share of the mix comes from Dedicated. It runs with more than 1,500 tractors and contracts with poultry, food, and industrial customers. This is where I have identified more CapEx and growth, since units increased +162 YoY, with the goal of building out activities that depend less on the cycle, such as poultry or food transportation.

Managed Freight is CVLG’s brokerage arm. The company subcontracts other carriers instead of using its own fleet. It also supports Expedited, absorbing loads when internal capacity is lacking.

In Q2, this business showed strong performance with a one-off contract. As long as it keeps margins around 5%, it is a profitable unit that requires little capital investment.

And the last vertical is Warehousing. It generates around $100M annually and provides support to the other verticals through storage and logistics.

Unlike peers such as Pamt Corp. (PAMT) or Universal Logistics (ULH), I see a revenue mix less dependent on a single sector, which works as a buffer in cycles without tailwinds.

Q2 2025: Record Revenue, Margins Under Pressure

Even though we are still in a weak cycle, CVLG posted record revenue of $302.9M this Q2, up 5.3% YoY. GAAP EPS came in lower than last year, but it still beat market expectations.

By business verticals, Dedicated contributed the most to the revenue mix with $102M, growing 9.4%. This is a positive sign, since the company’s CapEx is translating into higher sales.

Expedited fell 10%, bringing in $97M. This was due to a smaller fleet, as resources are being shifted into Dedicated, and to fewer miles per unit.

The surprise of the quarter came from Managed Freight, which delivered $77M thanks to a one-off contract. However, management made it clear on the earnings call that this client will not repeat in the second half of the year. Lastly, Warehousing added $25M and stayed flat.

That one-off Managed Freight contract also showed up in costs, as purchased transportation rose to $76.8M from $62.8M last year. This reflects the heavier use of subcontracted capacity, which is normal given the revenue recognized.

On insurance and claims, what stands out to me is that while quarterly expenses stayed flat at $17.3M, long-term provisions nearly doubled to $38.5M. This is a sign the company expects future claims and is something to watch.

To wrap up the quarter, the balance sheet closed with just $0.14M in cash and heavy reliance on the $110M revolver, of which $65.5M was still available. Even so, the company continued buying back stock, repurchasing $35M this quarter. That pushed net debt to $268.7M and leverage to 2x EBITDA.

My take on these results is that Covenant is showing good resilience in a weak cycle. The growth in Dedicated confirms that CapEx is paying off and that diversification is working. Margins remain tight, but beating expectations in this environment and growing stable contracts is, in my view, a solid outcome for the quarter.

As an addition, and looking ahead to the upcoming Q3 results, I expect them to come in weaker. Managed Freight will lose momentum after failing to retain the client, although Dedicated should continue to grow thanks to the fleet expansion, albeit with fewer miles per unit.

With diesel up 15% YTD and higher provisions set aside for claims, I believe the OR will remain above 95%. Even so, EPS should stay positive, though lower than the figure reported in Q2.

The rest should hold around current levels, with liquidity still relying on the revolver, leverage at 2x, and CapEx aimed at maintaining the fleet.

Valuation in Line With Peers

At today’s price of $23.88, CVLG trades at 6.4x EV/EBITDA TTM on about $142M of EBITDA. From what I see, that is in line with peers such as Heartland Express (HTLD) (6.6x), Universal Logistics (6x) and Marten Transport (MRTN) (6.9x), so there isn’t a clear discount that offsets current cycle risks.

The company has boosted EPS with aggressive buybacks, but it has done so at the cost of leaving cash at zero and increasing reliance on the revolver as its liquidity buffer. In my view, this isn’t concerning, since this leverage ratio is fairly normal for the sector.

With EBITDA around $140M at a 6x multiple, and subtracting net debt, the stock is roughly where it should be trading today. If expectations are beaten, one could see a multiple expansion toward 7x, which, with $150M EBITDA and net debt deducted, would imply a share price of about $31. That’s not far-fetched given the levels already seen in 2025.

In the short term, though, I don’t think the cycle will push hard enough. The sector index remains negative, I don’t see rates recovering, and rising costs continue to squeeze margins. By 2026 I’ll revisit the sector to see if there’s real recovery in pricing and volumes.

My current view is to hold if you already have a position, as Covenant is executing a solid strategy to create shareholder value, and there’s no reason, for now, to sell. If you don’t have exposure yet, it’s better to wait and start building a position once the sector shows tailwinds.

Risks: Concentration, Claims and Minimal Cash

Customer concentration is clearly the first risk I see. In the 2024 10-K, ten clients accounted for 45% of revenue. On top of that, one of them alone represented 10% of sales in both 2023 and 2024. These figures have stayed stable over time, but the loss of any of these contracts would have a meaningful impact on the P&L.

I also noticed a sharp increase in claims on the balance sheet, almost double. Operating under a self-insurance model, the company retains part of each loss before excess coverage kicks in. In practice, this means CVLG pays roughly $1M out of pocket per claim, and only after that does the insurer step in.

At year-end 2024, the liability line stood at $19M, and six months later it reached $38.5M. With margins already tight and cash close to zero, if accidents or claims rise, the company would have to rely on the revolver.

The debt/EBITDA ratio stands at 2x, so the financial risk is not critical, but it is something to monitor. CVLG generated $46.7M in operating cash flow, which is not enough to cover net CapEx of $52.8M. For now, the company seems able to sustain this effort through the revolver, but if claims do rise as suggested, or volumes fall, it would be forced to cut back on buybacks or increase leverage to secure liquidity.

After reviewing these risks, I don’t see an imminent covenant breach like in the PAMT Corp case, but the balance is delicate. Client concentration, rising claims on the balance sheet, and minimal cash all make this a point to watch closely.

Technical Block: Sideways Range and Support at $17.5

After peaking in 2023, the stock has been trading sideways in a range between $20 and $30. The $20 level also lines up with the 200-week moving average, while on the upside, the 50-week moving average is acting as near-term resistance around $25.

The RSI at 50 tells me the stock is in no man’s land. Buyers and sellers both look comfortable in this range, though the loss of the uptrend back in March 2024 coincided with mounting fundamental pressure and an EV/EBITDA multiple around 6x–6.5x (before the recent buybacks).

As long as it holds, the scenario is one of consolidation while waiting for catalysts, like an improvement in OR or a normalization of claims, that could push the stock back toward the top of the range, at $27–30. But if $20 breaks, the next level is $17.6, a historical support that would mean the market is starting to price margin pressure directly into EBITDA.

The technical picture reinforces my fundamental view. There are still no clear catalysts that would show an improvement in OR and justify a move back to the top of the valuation range. In other words, hold if you’re already in, or wait for the $17.5 level, a support that has held since 2015, to reassess.

A Stock to Withstand a Weak Cycle

I maintain a Hold rating on CVLG.

The reasons are clear: its operating ratio sits around 95%, leverage is 2x with cash at minimum levels, and the company depends on the revolver. On top of that, trading at 6.4x EV/EBITDA puts it right in line with peers.

For now, the cycle is not helping either, so I see the stock moving sideways between $17 and $26, at least in the short term.

CVLG is weathering the storm well, and it’s worth keeping on the watchlist for when tailwinds show up, like rate improvements that lift margins through pricing power.

But it’s still too early to build a position if you’re on the sidelines.
